cards.q: When does the cardiovascular system develop?
cards.a: From 18 days to 12 weeks — it is the earliest functional system in the body.

cards.q: Why does the CVS form so early?
cards.a: Because the embryo has grown too big for simple diffusion to meet oxygen and nutrient demands, so a delivery system is needed by about the 3rd week.

cards.q: List the five sources of the CVS.
cards.a: Splanchnic mesoderm; paraxial and lateral mesoderm; otic placodes; neural crest cells; and the primordium of the labyrinth of the internal ear.

cards.q: What does splanchnic mesoderm contribute to the heart?
cards.a: The primordium (foundation) of the heart — heart muscle and lining.

cards.q: What do the paraxial and lateral mesoderm contribute?
cards.a: Blood vessel walls and connective tissue.

cards.q: What do neural crest cells contribute?
cards.a: Outflow tract septation and the great vessel walls — defects here cause TGA, TOF and Truncus arteriosus.

cards.q: What are the two most important sources to remember and their products?
cards.a: Splanchnic mesoderm (heart muscle and lining) and neural crest cells (outflow tract).

cards.q: What are myoblasts?
cards.a: Cardiac progenitor cells (future heart-muscle cells) that cluster in the cardiogenic field.

cards.q: What are angioblastic cords?
cards.a: Cord-like structures formed by the myoblasts, which hollow out and fuse to form the primitive heart tube.

cards.q: Where is the cardiogenic field located?
cards.a: Anterior (in front) to the endodermal primitive pharynx, initially cephalad (near the head).

cards.q: Why does the heart end up in the chest despite starting cephalad?
cards.a: Because the embryo folds craniocaudally in weeks 3-4, carrying the heart down into the thoracic region.

cards.q: When does the primitive heart tube form and from what?
cards.a: By the 4th week, from the two angioblastic cords hollowing out and fusing into one straight tube.

cards.q: What anchors the caudal (lower) end of the heart tube?
cards.a: The septum transversum (a block of mesoderm that later helps form the diaphragm).

cards.q: What anchors the cephalad (upper) end of the heart tube?
cards.a: The 6 arterial arches.

cards.q: When does dilatation of the heart tube become visible?
cards.a: Days 21 to 24.

cards.q: What will the venous (caudal) end of the heart tube become?
cards.a: The atria.

cards.q: What will the arterial (cephalad) end of the heart tube become?
cards.a: The ventricles and outflow tract.

cards.q: Why does the heart tube loop?
cards.a: It is anchored at both ends in the limited pericardial cavity and growing rapidly with nowhere to elongate, so it bends and loops on itself.

cards.q: What abnormality results from looping in the wrong direction?
cards.a: Dextrocardia (heart on the wrong side).

cards.q: Describe stage 1 of looping (atrial looping).
cards.a: A right-angle bend between the primitive atrium and the interventricular region, driven by differential growth — the posterior-left grows slowly and the anterior-right grows fast.

cards.q: Describe stage 2 of looping (ventricular looping).
cards.a: A dramatic 180-degree flexure between the interventricular region and the outlet ventricle, after which the tube resembles the definitive heart.

cards.q: What does looping achieve overall?
cards.a: It repositions the atria behind and above the ventricles, with the ventricles anterior and inferior — the adult arrangement.

cards.q: What problem do convergence and wedging solve?
cards.a: After looping, the inlets and outlets are not yet aligned over their correct ventricles; convergence and wedging realign them before septation.

cards.q: What is convergence?
cards.a: The AV canal (inlet) and bulbus cordis (outlet) move closer toward each other into the same plane.

cards.q: Describe wedging.
cards.a: A two-part movement: the AV canal shifts to the right so each AV orifice sits over its ventricle, and the bulbus cordis shifts to the left so half the outlet sits over each ventricular outflow tract.

cards.q: After wedging, over which ventricles do the valves and great vessels sit?
cards.a: Tricuspid over the RV, mitral over the LV, aorta over the LV (wedged between the AV valves), and pulmonary trunk over the RV.

cards.q: What defect results from failed wedging?
cards.a: Double Outlet Right Ventricle (DORV) — both aorta and pulmonary trunk over the RV.

cards.q: What defect results from abnormal convergence?
cards.a: Double Inlet Left Ventricle (DILV) — both AV valves drain into the LV.

cards.q: Which three paired veins drain into the sinus venosus?
cards.a: The vitelline vein, the umbilical vein, and the common cardinal vein.

cards.q: What is the function and adult fate of the vitelline vein?
cards.a: Returns deoxygenated blood from the yolk sac; becomes the hepatic veins and portal vein.

cards.q: What is the function of the umbilical vein and why is it unusual?
cards.a: It carries oxygenated blood from the placenta to the embryo — unusual for a vein — because in fetal life the placenta is the lung.

cards.q: What is the adult fate of the umbilical vein?
cards.a: It forms the ductus venosus, which becomes the ligamentum venosum at birth.

cards.q: What is the role of the ductus venosus?
cards.a: It allows oxygenated blood from the placenta to bypass the liver and go directly to the heart; it closes at birth.

cards.q: What does the common cardinal vein return, and what does it form?
cards.a: Deoxygenated blood from the body; it forms the SVC and IVC.

cards.q: What two tributaries form each common cardinal vein?
cards.a: The anterior cardinal vein (drains the head) and the posterior cardinal vein (drains the lower body).

cards.q: What is the adult fate of the right anterior cardinal vein?
cards.a: It becomes the superior vena cava (SVC).

cards.q: What is the fate of the left anterior cardinal vein?
cards.a: It largely regresses.

cards.q: From how many segments is the IVC assembled, and name them.
cards.a: Four: hepatic veins, right subcardinal vein, right supracardinal vein, and the subcardinal-supracardinal anastomosis.

cards.q: Give examples of IVC anomalies from segmental failure.
cards.a: Absent infrarenal IVC, interrupted IVC with azygos continuation, and double IVC.

cards.q: What is the recurring theme of systemic venous development?
cards.a: Right-sided dominance — the right cardinal system forms the SVC and IVC while the left mostly regresses.

cards.q: Why can the atria not be completely sealed in fetal life?
cards.a: Because the fetus does not use its lungs; oxygenated placental blood must bypass the pulmonary circulation and reach the left heart via the foramen ovale.

cards.q: How does septum primum grow, and what gap does it leave?
cards.a: It grows down from the roof of the atrium toward the endocardial cushions; the gap below its free edge is the foramen primum.

cards.q: How does the foramen secundum form?
cards.a: As septum primum reaches the floor and closes the foramen primum, perforations appear higher up in septum primum and coalesce into the foramen secundum.

cards.q: Describe septum secundum.
cards.a: An invagination of the roof of the atrium growing down to the right of septum primum; it is thicker and more muscular, and stops short to leave the foramen ovale.

cards.q: Why is septum secundum thicker and more muscular than septum primum?
cards.a: To withstand the higher pressure and blood flow in the right atrium during fetal life.

cards.q: What is the foramen ovale?
cards.a: The gap left because septum secundum grows down but stops short of the endocardial cushions, not fully covering the foramen secundum.

cards.q: In fetal life, why is the foramen ovale open?
cards.a: Right atrial pressure exceeds left (lungs not working), pushing septum primum leftward and opening the foramen ovale so blood flows right to left.

cards.q: At birth, why does the foramen ovale close?
cards.a: The first breath expands the lungs, pulmonary pressure drops and left atrial pressure rises above the right, pushing septum primum rightward against septum secundum.

cards.q: What does the foramen ovale become in the adult?
cards.a: The fossa ovalis (after the two septa fuse).

cards.q: What are the endocardial cushions, and what three things do they contribute to?
cards.a: Masses of tissue in the AV canal region; they help close the foramen primum, contribute to the mitral and tricuspid valves, and contribute to the membranous part of the interventricular septum.

cards.q: What defect results from endocardial cushion failure, and which syndrome is it associated with?
cards.a: AV septal defect (AVSD), commonly associated with Down syndrome.

cards.q: What causes ASD secundum, the most common ASD?
cards.a: Excessive resorption of septum primum or a foramen ovale that is too large.

cards.q: What causes ASD primum?
cards.a: Failure of septum primum to fuse with the endocardial cushions.

cards.q: What causes a patent foramen ovale, and how common is it?
cards.a: The septa form but never permanently fuse; present in about 25% of adults.

cards.q: What causes a common atrium?
cards.a: Complete failure of atrial septation.

cards.q: What are the two horns of the sinus venosus and their broad fates?
cards.a: The right horn is absorbed into the right atrium; the left horn shrinks dramatically.

cards.q: What does the left sinus horn form?
cards.a: The coronary sinus and the oblique vein of the left atrium.

cards.q: What does the right sinus horn form?
cards.a: The smooth-walled sinus venarum of the right atrium and the valve of the IVC (Eustachian valve).

cards.q: What are the two parts of the adult right atrium and their origins?
cards.a: The rough/trabeculated part (pectinate muscles) from the primitive atrium, and the smooth part (sinus venarum) from the absorbed right sinus horn.

cards.q: What is the crista terminalis?
cards.a: The muscular ridge marking the internal boundary between the rough and smooth parts of the right atrium (externally, the sulcus terminalis).

cards.q: What is persistent left superior vena cava (PLSVC)?
cards.a: The left anterior cardinal vein fails to regress, leaving a left SVC that drains into the coronary sinus; it is the commonest thoracic venous anomaly and usually asymptomatic.

cards.q: What is a coronary sinus ASD?
cards.a: An unroofed coronary sinus open to the left atrium, creating a shunt.

cards.q: How does the left atrium get its smooth wall?
cards.a: By absorbing the pulmonary veins — the LA grows an outgrowth that meets and progressively absorbs the pulmonary veins, so one trunk becomes four openings.

cards.q: How many pulmonary vein openings result, and why?
cards.a: Four — as the original single trunk is absorbed, its branches become the four separate openings.

cards.q: What is the origin of the smooth wall of each atrium?
cards.a: Right atrium — from the absorbed right sinus horn; left atrium — from the absorbed pulmonary veins. Both have rough anterior walls from the primitive atrium.

cards.q: Where does the conduction system develop from?
cards.a: Specialised cells in the right atrium close to where the sinus venosus connected.

cards.q: What two structures does the conduction system form and their functions?
cards.a: The AV node (delays the atrial signal before the ventricles) and the Purkinje fibres (rapidly conduct through the ventricular walls).

cards.q: What is partial versus total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage?
cards.a: Partial APVD — some pulmonary veins drain to the wrong place (RA, SVC, IVC); Total APVD — all four drain abnormally so no oxygenated blood enters the LA directly.

cards.q: Why is total APVD a surgical emergency?
cards.a: Because oxygenated blood never reaches the left side of the heart properly.

cards.q: Why can sinus venosus defects cause arrhythmias?
cards.a: Because the conduction system develops from the sinus venosus region, so structural defects there disturb conduction tissue, causing sick sinus syndrome and heart block.

cards.q: How does ventricular septation differ from atrial septation in direction?
cards.a: In the atria the wall grows downward from the roof; in the ventricles the wall grows upward from the floor.

cards.q: From how many components is the ventricular septum assembled, and name them.
cards.a: Four: the inlet, the outlet, the trabecular/muscular part, and the membranous septum.

cards.q: Why is VSD the most common congenital heart defect?
cards.a: Because the ventricular septum is assembled from four separate components, giving four separate opportunities for something to go wrong.

cards.q: Describe the membranous septum and its clinical importance.
cards.a: It is the last part to close and the smallest, a tiny fibrous patch just below the aortic valve; it is responsible for the perimembranous VSD, about 80% of all VSDs.

cards.q: What is the cono-truncal region made of?
cards.a: Two stacked segments: the bulbus cordis (conus, lower) and the truncus arteriosus (upper).

cards.q: What must the cono-truncal region be divided into?
cards.a: The aorta (for the LV) and the pulmonary trunk (for the RV).

cards.q: Why does the cono-truncal septum grow in a spiral?
cards.a: Because in the adult heart the pulmonary trunk spirals around the aorta; the spiral septation produces that arrangement.

cards.q: What defect results from straight (non-spiral) cono-truncal division?
cards.a: Transposition of the Great Arteries (TGA) — aorta off the RV and pulmonary trunk off the LV.

cards.q: What is the role of neural crest cells in cono-truncal septation?
cards.a: They migrate into the region, populate the aorticopulmonary ridges, and are essential for proper fusion.

cards.q: What defects result from failed neural crest cell migration in the outflow tract?
cards.a: Truncus arteriosus and Tetralogy of Fallot.

cards.q: Why are outlet VSDs often associated with cono-truncal anomalies?
cards.a: The cono-truncal septum also grows down to close the outlet part of the interventricular septum, so a failure affects both the outflow division and the outlet septum.

cards.q: Give the mechanism of Truncus arteriosus.
cards.a: Complete failure of aorticopulmonary ridge fusion — a single common arterial trunk persists.

cards.q: Give the mechanism of Tetralogy of Fallot.
cards.a: Unequal division of the conus — the pulmonary side gets too little, causing pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, outlet VSD and RVH.

cards.q: Give the mechanism of Double Outlet RV.
cards.a: Abnormal wedging plus failure of neural crest cell migration — both great vessels over the RV.

cards.q: From what do the great vessels develop?
cards.a: Six paired arterial arches that form sequentially around the pharynx.

cards.q: What is the general fate of the aortic arches?
cards.a: Most regress (like scaffolding demolished after use), keeping only the parts needed for the adult arterial system.

cards.q: What does the 3rd aortic arch become?
cards.a: The common carotid artery, the external carotid artery, and the proximal (first part of the) internal carotid artery, bilaterally.

cards.q: What does the left 4th aortic arch become?
cards.a: Part of the arch of the aorta.

cards.q: What does the right 4th aortic arch become?
cards.a: The most proximal segment of the right subclavian artery.

cards.q: Why is the aortic arch left-sided in normal adults?
cards.a: Because only the left 4th arch persists to form it.

cards.q: What does the 5th aortic arch become?
cards.a: Nothing — it regresses completely.

cards.q: What does the left 6th aortic arch become?
cards.a: The proximal left pulmonary artery and, distally, the ductus arteriosus.

cards.q: What does the right 6th aortic arch become?
cards.a: The proximal segment of the right pulmonary artery only (the distal part regresses).

cards.q: What is the ductus arteriosus and its fate?
cards.a: The fetal shunt connecting the pulmonary trunk to the descending aorta, bypassing the fetal lungs; at birth it closes to become the ligamentum arteriosum.

cards.q: What is patent ductus arteriosus (PDA)?
cards.a: Failure of the ductus arteriosus (6th arch) to close after birth — one of the commonest congenital heart defects.

cards.q: What causes a right-sided aortic arch?
cards.a: The right 4th arch persists instead of the left.

cards.q: What is an aberrant right subclavian artery, and what symptom can it cause?
cards.a: A 4th arch and right dorsal aorta abnormality where the artery crosses behind the oesophagus, causing dysphagia lusoria.

cards.q: What is an interrupted aortic arch?
cards.a: A 4th arch defect producing a gap in the aortic arch.

cards.q: What is the embryological cause of dextrocardia?
cards.a: The heart tube loops to the left instead of the right — abnormal looping.

cards.q: What is the embryological cause of ASD?
cards.a: Failure of atrial septation.

cards.q: What is the embryological cause of VSD?
cards.a: Failure of ventricular septation.

cards.q: What is the embryological cause of transposition of the great arteries?
cards.a: Non-spiral (straight) division of the bulbus cordis — the conal growth hypothesis.

cards.q: What is the embryological cause of hypoplastic left heart syndrome?
cards.a: Failure of migration of the neural crest cells.

cards.q: What is the embryological cause of double inlet LV?
cards.a: Abnormal wedging and failure of neural crest cell migration.

cards.q: OSPE — On a diagram of the primitive heart tube, name the regions from the venous to the arterial end.
cards.a: Sinus venosus, primitive atrium, primitive ventricle, bulbus cordis, truncus arteriosus.

cards.q: OSPE — A diagram shows two atrial septa with an offset gap acting as a flap valve. Name the septa and the gap.
cards.a: Septum primum (thin flap) and septum secundum (thick muscular wall); the gap is the foramen ovale.

cards.q: OSPE — Identify the arch derivatives numbered 3, 4 and 6.
cards.a: 3rd = carotid system; 4th = aortic arch (left) / right subclavian (right); 6th = pulmonary arteries + ductus arteriosus (left).

cards.q: CLINICAL — A newborn with Down syndrome has a murmur and a single AV valve on echo. What is the defect and its embryological cause?
cards.a: AV septal defect (AVSD) — failure of endocardial cushion development.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A cyanotic newborn has the aorta arising from the right ventricle and the pulmonary trunk from the left. What is the diagnosis and cause?
cards.a: Transposition of the great arteries — straight (non-spiral) division of the cono-truncal region.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A cyanotic child has pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, VSD and RVH. What is the diagnosis and embryological cause?
cards.a: Tetralogy of Fallot — unequal conal division / failure of neural crest cell migration.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A term newborn is deeply cyanosed and no oxygenated blood reaches the left atrium. What is the likely diagnosis?
cards.a: Total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage (TAPVD) — a surgical emergency.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A preterm infant has a continuous murmur and a shunt between the pulmonary trunk and aorta. What is the defect and its arch origin?
cards.a: Patent ductus arteriosus — failure of the 6th arch ductus arteriosus to close.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which single cell type, when it fails to migrate, explains TGA, TOF and Truncus arteriosus?
cards.a: Neural crest cells.
